subUnits.title: 7.1 Meaning and Types of Environmental Hazards: Drought, Desert Encroachment, Pollution, Deforestation, Soil Erosion
notes: • Natural or human-induced events that pose risks to human life, property, and ecosystems
• Can be sudden (earthquakes, floods) or gradual (desertification, sea-level rise)
• Increasingly influenced by climate change and human activities
• Definition: Prolonged period of abnormally low rainfall leading to water shortage
- Water scarcity for domestic and industrial use
- Early warning systems and contingency planning
• Definition: Degradation of land in arid, semi-arid, and dry sub-humid areas
- Loss of fertile soil and reduced agricultural productivity
- Increased poverty and migration from affected areas
- Afforestation and reforestation (Great Green Wall initiative)
• Definition: Introduction of harmful substances or energy into the environment
- Vehicle emissions, generator fumes, industrial smoke
- Open burning of waste and biomass
- Health problems: respiratory diseases, cancer, waterborne illnesses
- Ecosystem damage: loss of biodiversity, soil degradation
- Economic costs: healthcare, cleanup, lost productivity
- Clean technologies and renewable energy adoption
• Definition: Permanent removal of forests for other land uses
- Fuelwood and charcoal production (major energy source)
- Loss of biodiversity and ecosystem services
- Soil erosion and reduced water quality
- Contribution to climate change (reduced carbon sequestration)
- Disruption of livelihoods for forest-dependent communities
- Alternative energy sources to reduce fuelwood dependence
• Definition: Removal of topsoil by water, wind, or human activities
- Gully erosion (severe in Southeast: Anambra, Enugu, Imo)
- Sheet and rill erosion (widespread in cultivated areas)
- Loss of fertile topsoil and reduced agricultural productivity
- Terracing, contour plowing, and cover cropping
- Proper drainage and erosion control structures
- Sustainable land use planning and policies
• Hazards often reinforce each other: deforestation → soil erosion → reduced water retention → drought
• Climate change exacerbates many hazards: more intense rainfall, longer dry spells
• Human vulnerability depends on poverty, governance, and adaptive capacity
• Niger Delta: Oil pollution, coastal erosion
• Urban areas: Air/water pollution, waste management challenges
• National responses: NESREA, Great Green Wall, climate change policies
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing causes and effects of drought: low rainfall → crop failure → food insecurity → migration
Map of Nigeria highlighting hazard-prone areas: desertification (North), gully erosion (Southeast), oil pollution (Delta)
Flowchart of deforestation impacts: tree removal → soil exposure → erosion → reduced fertility → more clearing
Pollution sources and pathways diagram: industry/vehicles → air/water/land → human/ecosystem health

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Environmental hazards drought desertification pollution Nigeria geography site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Drought is best defined as:
opts: Prolonged period of abnormally low rainfall leading to water shortage
quiz.explanation: Drought is a prolonged deficiency in precipitation that causes water shortage, affecting agriculture, ecosystems, and human activities.

quiz.q: Desert encroachment in Nigeria is mainly caused by:
quiz.explanation: Desertification results from human activities (overgrazing, deforestation) combined with climate variability, degrading dryland ecosystems.

quiz.q: A major effect of oil pollution in the Niger Delta is:
opts: Contamination of water and soil, harming livelihoods
quiz.explanation: Oil spills contaminate waterways and farmland, destroying fisheries, agriculture, and drinking water sources for local communities.

quiz.q: Deforestation contributes to climate change because:
opts: Forests store carbon; removing them releases CO₂ and reduces sequestration
quiz.explanation: Forests act as carbon sinks; deforestation releases stored carbon and reduces the planet's capacity to absorb CO₂.

quiz.q: Gully erosion is most severe in which part of Nigeria?
quiz.explanation: The Southeast's sandy soils, intense rainfall, and land use practices have led to severe gully erosion threatening communities.

quiz.q: A solution to soil erosion is:
quiz.explanation: Conservation practices like terracing, cover crops, and tree planting reduce soil exposure and hold soil in place.

quiz.q: Air pollution in Nigerian cities is mainly from:
opts: Vehicle emissions, generators, and industrial smoke
quiz.explanation: Urban air pollution stems from transport, power generation (generators), industry, and waste burning.

quiz.q: The Great Green Wall initiative aims to combat:
opts: Desertification in the Sahel through afforestation
quiz.explanation: The Great Green Wall is a pan-African effort to restore degraded land and combat desertification across the Sahel, including Northern Nigeria.

quiz.q: Environmental hazards are increasingly influenced by:
quiz.explanation: Human-induced climate change and land use changes intensify hazards like droughts, floods, and erosion.

quiz.q: Sustainable management of environmental hazards requires:
opts: Integrated approaches: policy, community action, technology, and awareness
quiz.explanation: Effective hazard management combines scientific knowledge, policy frameworks, community participation, and adaptive strategies.

subUnits.title: 7.2 Coastal Erosion, Flooding, and Deforestation: Causes, Effects, and Solutions
notes: • Definition: Wearing away of land and removal of beach sediments by wave action, currents, tides, and human activities
• Niger Delta coast (Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta states)
• Natural: Wave energy, sea-level rise, storm surges, geological subsidence
• Human: Sand mining, construction of ports/breakwaters, removal of mangroves, climate change
• Loss of land, property, and infrastructure
• Economic losses in tourism, fisheries, real estate
• Hard engineering: Sea walls, groynes, breakwaters
• Soft engineering: Beach nourishment, mangrove restoration
• Climate adaptation planning and early warning systems
• Definition: Overflow of water onto normally dry land
• Riverine flooding: From overflowing rivers (Niger, Benue, their tributaries)
• Coastal flooding: From storm surges, sea-level rise
• Urban flooding: From poor drainage, impervious surfaces, waste blockage
• Flash floods: Sudden, intense rainfall in small catchments
• Natural: Heavy rainfall, cyclones, sea-level rise, geological factors
• Human: Deforestation (reduces infiltration), urbanization (impervious surfaces), poor drainage, waste dumping, dam operations
• Structural: Dams, levees, drainage systems, floodways
• Non-structural: Land use planning, early warning systems, insurance
• Community preparedness: Evacuation plans, emergency response
• Definition: Permanent conversion of forest land to non-forest uses
• Agricultural expansion: Shifting cultivation, commercial plantations (oil palm, rubber)
• Fuelwood and charcoal: ~70% of Nigerians rely on biomass for cooking
• Logging: Legal and illegal timber extraction
• Infrastructure: Roads, settlements, mining, oil exploration
• Population pressure and poverty driving land conversion
• Biodiversity loss: Nigeria has lost ~90% of its primary rainforest
• Climate impacts: Reduced carbon sequestration, altered rainfall patterns
• Soil degradation: Erosion, nutrient depletion, reduced water retention
• Livelihood impacts: Loss of non-timber forest products, cultural values
• Increased vulnerability to floods and landslides
• Policy and governance: Strengthen forest laws, combat illegal logging
• Community forestry: Empower local communities in management
• Alternative livelihoods: Agroforestry, eco-tourism, sustainable NTFP harvesting
• Energy transition: Promote LPG, solar cookers to reduce fuelwood demand
• Climate Change Link: Hazards intensify with warming; mitigation (reducing emissions) and adaptation (building resilience) are both needed
• Poverty and Vulnerability: Poor communities are most exposed and least able to cope
• Governance: Effective institutions, transparency, and community participation are critical
• Integrated Approaches: Address root causes (e.g., poverty, energy access) rather than just symptoms
• National Environmental Standards and Regulations Enforcement Agency (NESREA)
• National Climate Change Policy and Response Strategy
• Erosion and Watershed Management Project (World Bank-supported)
• Challenges: Implementation gaps, funding, coordination, data
diagramDescriptions: Coastal erosion process diagram: waves → sediment transport → land loss → human impacts
Flooding types map of Nigeria: riverine (Niger/Benue), coastal (Delta), urban (Lagos, Kano)
Deforestation drivers flowchart: population → demand → land conversion → forest loss → impacts
Solutions matrix: structural vs non-structural vs ecosystem-based approaches for each hazard

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Coastal erosion flooding deforestation Nigeria geography WAEC site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Coastal erosion in Nigeria is most severe in:
quiz.explanation: Lagos and Niger Delta coasts experience significant erosion due to wave action, sea-level rise, and human activities like sand mining.

quiz.q: A major human cause of coastal erosion is:
opts: Sand mining and removal of mangroves
quiz.explanation: Sand mining for construction and mangrove clearance for development remove natural coastal defenses, accelerating erosion.

quiz.q: Urban flooding in Nigerian cities is often worsened by:
opts: Poor drainage and waste dumping blocking channels
quiz.explanation: Impervious surfaces, inadequate drainage, and solid waste blocking waterways exacerbate flooding during rains.

quiz.q: A structural solution to flooding is:
opts: Building dams, levees, and drainage systems
quiz.explanation: Structural measures physically control or divert floodwaters, complementing non-structural approaches like planning and early warning.

quiz.q: Deforestation in Nigeria's rainforests is mainly driven by:
quiz.explanation: Clearing forests for crops and harvesting trees for fuelwood are the dominant drivers of forest loss in Southern Nigeria.

quiz.q: A major effect of deforestation is:
opts: Loss of biodiversity and increased erosion
quiz.explanation: Forest loss reduces habitat for species and removes root systems that hold soil, leading to erosion and landslides.

quiz.q: Mangrove restoration helps combat coastal erosion because:
opts: Their roots stabilize sediments and reduce wave energy
quiz.explanation: Mangrove roots trap sediments and dissipate wave energy, protecting shorelines naturally and cost-effectively.

quiz.q: Climate change exacerbates environmental hazards by:
opts: Causing more intense rainfall, longer droughts, and sea-level rise
quiz.explanation: Climate change intensifies the hydrological cycle, leading to more extreme weather events that worsen floods, droughts, and coastal hazards.

quiz.q: Community-based natural resource management is important because it:
opts: Empowers local people who depend on resources, improving sustainability
quiz.explanation: Local communities have traditional knowledge and direct stakes in resource health; involving them improves compliance and outcomes.

quiz.q: Integrated approaches to environmental hazards are needed because:
opts: Hazards are interconnected and require multi-sectoral solutions
quiz.explanation: Hazards like deforestation, erosion, and flooding reinforce each other; effective management requires coordinated policies across sectors and scales.
